dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for unknown problem/person down in Richmond.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
The man is unconscious, laying on the sidewalk. I found him a few minutes ago.
He was breathing, but not waking up when I spoke to him. He smells like alcohol.
Can you open your eyes for us, sir?
(No response)
Sir, is there any way you can give us a response? Please squeeze my hand if you can hear us.
He's not responding to verbal or painful stimuli and currently appears unconscious but continues to breathe.
Partner, can we assess his pupils?
Yes, his pupils are pinpoint bilaterally but reactive.
Sir, we're going to place a nasal airway to help get air in and out. Is that okay with you?
Nasal airway placed in the left nare successfully.
Is there any obstruction or difficulty maintaining an airway?
Airway is open. I am not hearing any suspicious sounds.
We're going to be placing an ETCO2 monitor to assess ventilation. Is that okay with you, sir?
SpO2 is currently at 90% on room air. ETCO2 is 38.
Are there any changes in breathing effort or pattern?
Breathing is shallow and at a rate of 8 per minute.
We're going to use a nasal cannula at 4 liters per minute to improve oxygen saturation.
We're going to be taking your pulse and blood pressure now. Is that okay with you, sir?
Pulse is 130 beats per minute. Strong and regular.
Blood pressure is 126 over 83 mmHg.
Are there any signs of abnormal skin color, temperature, or signs of trauma?
Skin is warm, dry, and normal in color.
Partner, let's set up the IV access. We need to prepare in case Narcan becomes necessary.
IV access is being attempted in the left AC-first attempt unsuccessful.
Partner, let's attempt the right forearm for IV insertion.
IV established in the right forearm successfully, 18g.
Sir, we're going to give you a small medication through this IV. It's to see if we can wake you up safely. That's okay, right?
Can you tell me what signs and symptoms brought you down this way, sir?
I don't know. I just remember drinking a lot earlier. Then I woke up outside, wasn't sure how I got here.
Do you have any allergies we should be aware of?
I don't think so. I can't remember if I do.
Are you taking any medications regularly or have you taken any in the last few hours?
I don't have any. I don't take medicine.
What's your medical history?
I don't really have any. I tried once but it made me sick.
Have you had any surgeries or operations in the past?
No surgeries I can recall.
Sir, when was your last time eating or drinking?
I had a few drinks last night, that's it.
I don't think he's been doing drugs. He just looked like he passed out. Smelled strong of alcohol and sweat.
How old are you, sir?
55 years old.
Can you tell us your name?
James.
On a scale of 1-10, with 10 being the worst pain you've ever felt, how would you rate your pain, Mr. James?
Well, I don't feel any pain.
Can you give me a sense of your memory? For instance, do you know where we are right now?
I know I'm outside, that's it. I can't remember much.
And can you describe what happened, in your words, just now before you felt this way?
I think I just passed out. That's all I remember.
Great, thank you for that.
No bite marks, rashes, or scrapes beyond the elbow abrasion.
I don't see any signs of jaundice or paleness.
Mr. James, can you feel this? I'm going to lightly touch your left arm. Can you feel me?
Yeah, I can feel that.
Thank you. How about your right leg? Can you point your toe?
(Patient attempts and succeeds.) Yeah, I can.
Blood glucose is at 100.
Respiration rate is at 8 per minute and shallow.
EKG shows sinus tachycardia.
We are going to administer Narcan via IV push, followed by a 10mL saline flush. This is to reverse potential opioid effects.
Administered Narcan 0.5 mg IV. Initiated saline flush.
4 lead ECG monitoring initiated per ePCR at 20:51.
Post Narcan, blood pressure is 100 over 74.
Pulse is 97.
Respiration rate is 16.
SpO2 has improved to 99%.
GCS improved from 3 to 14 post Narcan. His verbal and motor responses are now more appropriate.
We stayed within Protocol 7-1-Medical-Overdose/Poisoning-Opioid. Narcan was administered per standing order.
The Narcan given was appropriate per our documentation and clinical findings.
On a scale of 1-10, how is your pain now?
Same as before-1, if there is any.
Just repeating, did you feel anything unusual before you felt this way?
No, I was just drinking a lot earlier. I don't know what else happened.
Mr. James, we're going to be taking you to Chippenham Emergency for further evaluation. Is that okay with you?
Whatever is best. I just want to be okay.
We're securing you to the stretcher using a five-point harness for your safety.
We're rolling the patient onto the stretcher using a horizontal non-emergent technique.
Based on vitals, current responsiveness, and initial field assessment, he's improving from alcohol-related intoxication and opioid suspicion.
We're closely observing for any changes in mental status or distress. Per report, he remains stable.
I'll relay your full set of vitals and mode of treatment to the receiving nurse. They'll take it from here.
All care has been documented on this patient. Any follow-ups are up to the ED team.
We've completed Narcan administration. No additional meds or interventions performed in the field.
Documented and noted the improvement to GCS 14 and vitals trending downward.
Your EKG showed sinus tach. We placed two IVs. First one failed, second one holds. Monitor hands for response and keep educated on his alcohol use.
Full report complete, including Narcan, IV, vitals, and a summary from bystander observations and patient history.
Mr. James was awake and relatively normal in GCS during transport. No changes in mental status reported.
Our field impression is abuse of alcohol with some suspicion of opioid use, but initial symptoms and history point more to alcohol intoxication.
The transport process has been smooth. All gripping points are secure, and the stretcher is labeled for attention at the hospital.
Vitals remain stable-BP at 100/74, pulse at 97, SpO2 at 99%, and all other signs trending well.
We'll continue to monitor in case of a delayed adverse response from Narcan, but all signs thus far are going well.
Alright, Chippenham Hospital is the receiving facility. Staff has been informed and ready to process him.
We note all IV lines are secure and in place, and patient has been placed in cravat restraint per protocol to help control movement post Narcan.
Transfer to bed 24 has been confirmed. Nurse has accepted and received all patient details.
Full report complete, including Narcan, IV, vitals, and a summary from bystander observations and patient history.
No signs of abdominal injury or pain noted based on findings in the field.
NPA in place for airway support during transport.
Extremities are in cravat restraint per protocol. All securing checks have been performed.
Full documentation of 4 lead ECG consistent with ePCR and field actions.
One more time, Mr. James, the officer found you with an odor of alcohol and no other trauma. Was that in line with your memories?
Yeah, I just talked to someone like that earlier. Can you let them know I'm safe?
Absolutely, they already know you're stable and being processed safely. Thank you for your cooperation today.
Don't hesitate if you need anything when you get inside. We'll make sure they know you're here for follow-up.
All medications, assessments, and vitals have been properly recorded in ePCR as per protocol.
Patient denies dizziness or any movement-related issues, consistent with documentation.
All assessments and vitals are in line with readiness for admission at Chippenham.
